channel.title: The Grant Desk
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk.

item.title: Stichting Stokroos: Residency at Fundaziun Nairs 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Stichting Stokroos

Location: Residency in Scuol, Switzerland (Fundaziun Nairs). Applicants must live and work in the Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname for at least one year. Applicants outside the Randstad particularly encouraged.

Award: Fully supported stay (accommodation and meals paid directly to Fundaziun Nairs) plus an unrestricted living, travel and production budget of EUR 7,000. 80% paid before the residency, the remainder on receipt of a brief final report. No application fee.

Duration: Intensive three-month working period (July through September).

Deadline: 11 Sep 2026

Stichting Stokroos funds a three-month reflective residency at Fundaziun Nairs in Scuol, Switzerland, for one selected visual artist, designer or craft practitioner (overlap possible). Both emerging and established makers may apply. Stokroos covers full accommodation and meals directly with Fundaziun Nairs and provides a discretionary EUR 7,000 budget for living, travel and production. The application should include a proposal that engages specifically with the residency, its Alpine surroundings and/or its historical and cultural context. Eligibility: you completed your first subject-specific (vocational or higher) programme in 2022 or earlier, or have an equivalent established practice, or started without formal training (first presentation marks the start of your practice); at least 21 years old; living and working in the Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname for at least one year; not currently enrolled in a study or post-academic programme. Applications are individual only; duos and collectives cannot apply. Makers already selected by Fundaziun Nairs may still apply if they meet the criteria. You may submit only one application per year across all Stokroos grants, and must not have received a Seed Grant rejection in the previous calendar year. Apply via the online form (Dutch or English) with a portfolio PDF up to 20 pages, optional video up to 10 minutes total, CV up to 2 pages, and work/plan descriptions up to 2,500 characters each. Selection includes an introductory interview with up to three candidates; Stokroos aims to respond within 8 weeks. No appeal is possible. Application window: 1 to 11 September 2026. More information at nairs.ch.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Unruly Mirrors Open Call 2026 (East and Central Asia, Feminist Art)
item.description:
Organisation: Unruly Mirrors

Location: East and Central Asia (Kazakhstan, Japan, South Korea, Mainland China, Hong Kong, Uzbekistan, Mongolia)

Award: Financial support details not publicly confirmed at time of entry. No application fee confirmed.

Duration: Not publicly confirmed.

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Unruly Mirrors is an open call for artists based in Kazakhstan, Japan, South Korea, Mainland China, Hong Kong, Uzbekistan, or Mongolia. The project examines beauty practices in East and Central Asia as spaces of norm reproduction, intimacy, and feminist resistance. Financial support and full eligibility criteria were not publicly confirmed at time of entry (the website was unreachable). Deadline: 15 July 2026. Apply or inquire at https://unrulymirrors.com. NOTE: verify financial support and full details directly before applying.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: BFI Doc Society: Features Production Fund (UK, Documentary)
item.description:
Organisation: BFI Doc Society

Location: UK

Award: Up to £150,000 non-recoupable grant. No application fee.

Duration: Feature-length non-fiction films for theatrical release; rolling two-stage process (EOI then full application)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The BFI Doc Society Features Production Fund (delivered via BFI with Doc Society as delegate partner) supports UK filmmakers producing non-fiction feature films intended for theatrical release. Two strands: Directions (first- or second-time directors) and Momentum (established talent). AMOUNT: up to £150,000 non-recoupable grant (the total Doc Society contribution per project cannot exceed £150,000). ELIGIBILITY: UK-based filmmakers, both emerging and established directors; experienced and debut producers eligible; projects must meet at least three of six stated priorities: equity/diversity, audience impact, talent development, creative risk, UK-wide access, and environmental sustainability. PROCESS: two-stage — submit an Expression of Interest, then a Full Application if invited. Rolling intake year-round. Opened May 2026. APPLY via BFI Doc Society website. Contact: hellobfi@docsociety.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Grants for Applied Work 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Longview Philanthropy

Location: Worldwide / remote (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)

Award: Typical grants $50,000–$2,000,000 per project; proposals outside this range considered when the work justifies it. May include some operational support for new organisations. No application fee.

Duration: Project-based; duration varies by scope.

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Longview Philanthropy invites proposals for applied work on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, or experiences of AI systems. Typical grants are $50,000–$2,000,000 per project, with proposals outside that range considered when justified. New organisations may also receive some operational support. ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES: seed funding for new organisations (think tanks, research bodies, initiatives); mentorship programmes for researchers entering the digital minds field; internships, new hires, or focused programmes on digital minds at existing AI organisations, academic labs, or policy institutions; convenings and field-building (workshops, conferences, summits, working groups addressing technical and governance questions). AREAS OF INTEREST: measuring AI welfare; consciousness and sentience research; governance and legal/social science frameworks for potentially sentient AI; communications work for thoughtful public discourse; research beyond the US; support organisations and operations professionals for independent technical researchers. SELECTION CRITERIA: potential impact, track record, demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relationship to AI risk, cost-effectiveness, legal compliance. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Revitalizing Rituals 2026 Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: Homo Faber Alapítvány (Homo Faber Foundation)

Location: Bhar Lazreg, Tunisia and Somogy County, Hungary; multi-stage in-person residency across both sites.

Award: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. No application fee.

Duration: Multi-stage residency: Placemaking Camp (1 week, November 2026, Hungary); Storytellers Camp (1 week, March 2027, Tunisia); Residency Period (minimum 1 month, April-July 2027); public presentation (August 2027); Closing Event (3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary).

Deadline: 19 Jul 2026

Revitalizing Rituals invites artists from all disciplines -- sound, movement, visual arts, installation, performance, and transdisciplinary practices -- to join a research-creation journey across two landscapes: Bhar Lazreg in Tunisia and the wetlands of Somogy County in Hungary. The project explores rituals as living forms of belonging: reconnecting communities, land, memory, food, ecological reciprocity, and the more-than-human world. Selected artists work with local communities as co-participants in a shared inquiry into moving from extraction toward reciprocity. Organised by Homo Faber Alapítvány (Hungary). AMOUNT: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. ELIGIBILITY: minimum 5 years of active artistic practice; portfolio reflecting interest in situated or relational work; no formal degree required; working language is English (local language navigation valued); must be available for the full residency cycle including all international travel; must be able to legally receive the fee and invoice the organising partners from country of residence. Strongly encourages applications from historically marginalised or underrepresented voices. KEY DATES: Application Deadline 19 July 2026 (midnight) -- note the open call PDF also references a 5 July date; the officially promoted deadline is 19 July; Online Q&A 29 June 2026, 14:00 Tunisia / 15:00 CET (register at https://forms.gle/brnZvXvFe4JB8zL26); Online Conversations with shortlisted artists: mid July to mid August 2026; Selection Results: 31 August 2026; Placemaking Camp: 1 week, end of November 2026, Hungary; Storytellers Camp: 1 week, March 2027, Tunisia; Residency Period: minimum 1 month (splittable), April to July 2027; Final Presentation: August 2027; Closing Event: 3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary. Apply via the open call form linked at https://homofaber.hu/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Artist-Open-Call-FINAL-in-the-RR-form.pdf.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Wheeler Centre: The Next Chapter 2027
item.description:
Organisation: The Wheeler Centre

Location: Melbourne, Australia; includes residency at Varuna, The National Writers' House, and an end-of-year publishing intensive at The Wheeler Centre.

Award: AUD $15,000 stipend per fellow. No application fee.

Duration: 12 months.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Next Chapter is The Wheeler Centre's flagship writing program, designed to elevate Australian stories that are underrepresented and to nurture a new wave of writers to tell them. Each fellow receives a AUD $15,000 stipend and is paired with a handpicked mentor for a twelve-month period to progress a major writing project. Fellows also receive a residency at Varuna, The National Writers' House; an end-of-year publishing intensive at The Wheeler Centre; and meaningful opportunities to connect with peers, agents, publishers, and readers. One fellowship place is dedicated specifically to a poet: The Malcolm Robertson Fellowship. 2027 judges: Melissa Lucashenko, Micaela Sahhar, Cher Tan, and Melanie Saward. ELIGIBILITY: Australian writers. No specific genre restrictions stated; the program focuses on stories that are not currently being told and voices that are underrepresented. TIMELINE: submissions open 2 June 2026; submissions close 31 July 2026 (5pm); 2027 Next Chapter Fellows announced 10 November 2026. Questions: thenextchapter@wheelercentre.com. Apply at https://www.wheelercentre.com/the-next-chapter.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Jamverse Jam (Protocolized worldbuilding contest)
item.description:
Organisation: Protocolized (Summer of Protocols)

Location: Online / Worldwide

Award: One USD 1,000 grand prize plus at least 10 additional USD 200 prizes; winning artifacts are published on the Jamverse website. No entry fee.

Duration: One-off contest; submit up to three artifacts by 31 July 2026

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Protocolized's fourth contest invites artists and writers (storytellers, poets, comic artists) to build, extend, and connect the Jamverse, a shared near-future science fiction universe of four intersecting story cycles (The Stockton Chronicles, Legends and Ledgers, UET-Trainverse, and Zoothesia). ELIGIBILITY: open to anyone; you may submit up to three in-universe worldbuilding artifacts, and the same creator can win multiple prizes. REQUIREMENTS: each artifact must be under 1,000 words (200-700 is the sweet spot) or a visual equivalent (comic panel, poster, advertisement, etc.) and must include at least one element drawn from one of the four sub-world 'toy boxes', a surprising 'traffic jam' (an emergent, ah-ha consequence of a technology or protocol), and a relation to the theme of Stigmergy (indirect coordination through modifying a shared environment). Artifact types can include comic panels, job postings, poems, forum threads, historic documents, or encyclopedia entries. APPLY: submit each artifact as a public Google Doc via the contest form, including the artifact, a one-paragraph explanation of the worlds/toy-box components used and how the piece relates to stigmergy and explores a traffic jam, and, if AI was used, documentation of your AI/LLM protocol. PRIZES: one USD 1,000 grand prize and at least 10 USD 200 prizes, all published on the Jamverse website. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026. Contest rules, the current Jamverse stories, and starter materials are on the Jamverse website.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: WBSO: R&D Tax Credit (rolling, Netherlands)
item.description:
Organisation: Rijksdienst voor Ondernemend Nederland (RVO)

Location: Netherlands. Dutch companies and self-employed paying payroll or income tax that conduct qualifying R&D.

Award: Payroll/income tax reduction for R&D, not a cash grant. 2026: 36 percent of the first EUR 380,000 of R&D wage costs (50 percent for startups), 16 percent above; fixed deduction for self-employed. National budget EUR 1.817 billion for 2026. No application fee.

Duration: Applied for in advance per period; cannot start before approval.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The WBSO (Wet Bevordering Speur- en Ontwikkelingswerk) is the Dutch R&D tax credit administered by RVO. It reduces payroll/wage tax for companies with R&D staff and provides an income-tax deduction for self-employed people doing R&D; it is a tax credit, not a cash grant. For 2026 the benefit is 36 percent of the first EUR 380,000 of R&D wage and other costs (50 percent for qualifying startups) and 16 percent above that; self-employed receive a fixed deduction (and need at least 500 R&D hours per year). Eligible applicants are Dutch companies and self-employed people paying payroll or income tax who carry out qualifying R&D in the EU. Applications are made in advance via Mijn RVO and cannot be retroactive; for coverage of the final three months of a year the deadline is 30 September. Time-keeping and project administration are required. No application fee.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Cultuur+Ondernemen: Cultuurlening (rolling loan, Netherlands)
item.description:
Organisation: Cultuur+Ondernemen

Location: Netherlands. Creative and cultural professionals and organisations residing and working in NL.

Award: Loan (must be repaid), up to EUR 500,000. Interest 2 percent up to EUR 50,000, then 2-5 percent (risk-based) above EUR 50,000; term up to 10 years. This is a LOAN, not a grant. No application fee.

Duration: Loan term up to 10 years; rolling.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Cultuurlening from Cultuur+Ondernemen is a low-interest loan (not a grant) for creative and cultural professionals and organisations in the Netherlands, to invest in their practice or business. Loans run up to EUR 500,000, with interest of 2 percent up to EUR 50,000 and 2-5 percent (risk-based) above EUR 50,000, and a term of up to 10 years. The loan must be repaid. Eligible applicants reside and work in the Netherlands and are either art-school graduates or have at least three years of professional practice (others can apply with a motivation and reference). Applications are accepted on a rolling basis with no fixed deadline. No application fee. Note: separate regional products exist (for example the Rotterdam and Amsterdam Cultuurlening) with different rates and caps.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: TBA21-Academy: Tentacular Fellowship 2026-2027 (Ocean Space, Venice)
item.description:
Organisation: TBA21-Academy, with NICHE Centre for Environmental Humanities and the UNESCO Chair on Water, Heritage and Sustainable Development at Ca' Foscari University

Location: Ocean Space and Ca' Foscari University, Venice, Italy; situated work in and with the communities of the Lagoon of Venice.

Award: Total fellowship support EUR 26,000. Phase 1 (incubation): EUR 3,000 grant for independent research and initiative development. Phase 2 (activation): EUR 8,000 grant plus a EUR 15,000 activation budget. No application fee.

Duration: 15 months, September 2026 to November 2027

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

TBA21-Academy, in collaboration with NICHE Centre for Environmental Humanities and the UNESCO Chair on Water, Heritage and Sustainable Development at Ca' Foscari University, is launching the first edition of the Tentacular Fellowship, an initiative-building fellowship based at Ocean Space and at Ca' Foscari in Venice. Conceived as a long-term platform for artistic research and ecological engagement, Ocean Space works to develop new forms of action capable of moving beyond awareness-raising to produce real, tangible territorial effects through situated collaboration with the communities, practitioners, researchers and civic actors already working within the Lagoon of Venice. The fellowship invites a practitioner or collective to initiate, consolidate or expand a situated initiative in the Lagoon through the creation of a Tentacular Unit: a collaborative, interdisciplinary platform able to gather artists, students, researchers, institutions, local communities and other actors around a specific socio-environmental challenge. Through situated research, alliance-building and public-facing formats, the fellow helps build the social, institutional and ecological conditions for the initiative to take root and grow over time. STRUCTURE: the two-year fellowship runs in two phases. In Phase 1 (incubation) the fellow is awarded a EUR 3,000 grant to support independent research and initiative development. In Phase 2 (activation) the fellow is awarded a EUR 8,000 grant plus a EUR 15,000 activation budget, for total fellowship support of EUR 26,000. Details on the application process, evaluation criteria and frequently asked questions are in the full open-call document. Application deadline: 5 July 2026, 11:59pm CET. Apply via the online form linked from the open-call page.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Stichting Stokroos: Maker Initiatives Grant 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Stichting Stokroos

Location: Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname; must have lived and worked there at least one year. Applicants outside the Randstad, or plans taking place there, are particularly encouraged.

Award: Fixed grant of EUR 25,000 or EUR 40,000 (chosen depending on need). Maximum participant fee EUR 2,200/month (40-hour week); third-party hire capped at EUR 85/hour excl. VAT. Co-funding not required. No application fee.

Duration: Plan starts in the next calendar year (2027) and runs a maximum of two years.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Stichting Stokroos Maker Initiatives supports established visual artists, designers and craft practitioners (overlap possible), including duos and collectives, in developing and realising a multivocal artistic project or programme centred on a common concern, in collaboration with other makers or communities. There is one application round per year and Stokroos can support 4 to 6 proposals; a maximum of 40 eligible applications are considered per period. Eligibility: you completed your first subject-specific (vocational or higher) programme in 2020 or earlier, or have an equivalent established track record, or started without formal training (first presentation marks the start of your practice); at least 21 years old; living and working in the Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname for at least one year; not currently enrolled in a study or post-academic programme. You must apply together with an artistic or creative partner (from professional makers to communities); duos and collectives must also have an external partner. You may submit only one application per year across all Stokroos grants, and must not have received a Seed Grant rejection in the previous calendar year. The grant is a fixed contribution of EUR 25,000 or EUR 40,000 depending on need. Plans can take place anywhere, start in the next calendar year and last a maximum of two years. Applications are submitted via the online form in Dutch or English (portfolio PDF up to 20 pages/person, optional video up to 10 minutes total, CV up to 2 pages/person, budget up to 3 pages, work/plan/collaboration descriptions up to 2,500 characters each). Stokroos aims to respond within 10 weeks; no appeal is possible. Application window: 20 to 31 July 2026. Questions: mail@stokroos.nl.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Pioneer Works: 2027 Open Call – Visual Arts and Music Residency (Brooklyn, NY)
item.description:
Organisation: Pioneer Works

Location: Pioneer Works, Red Hook, Brooklyn, New York, USA

Award: Visual Arts: USD 5,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 5 months). Music: USD 3,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 1 month). Free studio space and facilities access. No housing or travel provided. No application fee.

Duration: Visual Arts: 5 months. Music: 1 month. Cohort begins after jury decisions in late September 2026.

Deadline: 13 Jul 2026

Pioneer Works in Red Hook, Brooklyn invites applications from emerging visual artists and musicians for its 2027 residency cohort. 12 Visual Arts residents (5-month residency) and 12 Music residents (1-month residency) are selected each year through a two-phase jury process. WHAT IS PROVIDED: free studio space; access to facilities including risograph printer, darkroom, ceramics studio, large-format printer, 3D printers, thermobinder, and laser cutter; biweekly studio visits with curators, gallerists, critics, and artists; free access to all public programmes at Pioneer Works, including Second Sundays (monthly open studios and performances). FINANCIAL AWARD: USD 5,000 for Visual Arts residents; USD 3,000 for Music residents (each comprising a USD 2,500 honorarium plus USD 500/month for the residency duration); applicants' choice on how to spend it. Pioneer Works does not provide housing, travel, or visa sponsorship. ELIGIBILITY: open locally, nationally, and internationally; collectives may apply (resources shared); current students and programme alumni are not eligible. REVIEW PROCESS: phase one is internal scoring on technical ability, uniqueness of vision, and future potential (nontraditional career paths given special consideration); highest scorers advance to an external jury of art professionals and alumni who assess diversity of demographics and media, genuine studio need, and fit with Pioneer Works' public-facing culture. Jury convenes September 2026; all applicants notified by late September. Supported by Dalio Philanthropies, the Andy Warhol Foundation, NYC Department of Cultural Affairs, New York State Council on the Arts, and the National Endowment for the Arts. APPLY: via Pioneer Works website by 13 July 2026. Questions: residency@pioneerworks.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Women Photograph + Leica Project Grants 2026 (Documentary Photography)
item.description:
Organisation: Women Photograph (supported by Leica USA)

Location: Remote (international)

Award: Three grants of USD 10,000 each (USD 30,000 total). No application fee.

Duration: Project-based (new or in-progress photography projects).

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

Women Photograph + Leica Project Grants 2026 awards three grants of USD 10,000 each to support documentary photography projects. ELIGIBILITY: open to women, nonbinary, transgender, and intersex documentary photographers of any nationality; no membership required; no restrictions on camera equipment or career stage. Funding covers hard costs of reporting, photographers' creative fees, and any other expenses that support the production of new or in-progress still photography projects. Not intended for books, exhibitions, or workshops. Grantees retain full work ownership. One submission per applicant; applications in English (Google Translate acceptable); any use of generative AI must be disclosed. Applications opened 20 May 2026; deadline 30 June 2026 at 11:59 PM ET. Apply at https://app.picter.com/contests/bad7ea97-7b0d-4a9c-9e85-38e73529d62b/start.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Nakanojo Biennale 2027: International Artist Open Call (Gunma Prefecture, Japan)
item.description:
Organisation: Nakanojo Biennale Executive Committee / Nakanojo Town, Gunma Prefecture

Location: Nakanojo Town, Gunma Prefecture, Japan

Award: Production grant provided (amount not specified publicly). No exhibition fee, no residency fee. Insurance subsidies available. Artists responsible for materials, food, transportation, and installation/removal costs. No application fee.

Duration: Exhibition: 18 September – 24 October 2027 (37 days). Production period: April–August 2027. Residency accommodation available from April 2027.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

Nakanojo Biennale 2027 (11th edition) is an international open call for artists of all genres — sculpture, painting, photography, video art, installation, performance, and more — to exhibit in Nakanojo Town, Gunma Prefecture, Japan. Theme: 'Echoing Green', inspired by William Blake's poem, exploring cycles of life, regeneration, and connection between past and future generations. Open internationally to individual artists and artist groups at all career stages; new participants are encouraged. A production grant is provided (amount not publicly specified); no exhibition or residency fees are charged. Residency accommodation available from April 2027. Staff support provided for venue selection and installation guidance. First-time participants in Japan must attend an on-site venue inspection; an online orientation on 5 September 2026 is required for all accepted artists. Deadline: 12 July 2026 at 23:59 JST (online). Apply via Google Form at https://forms.gle/SS8DaaGA6uwUKogj7. Full details at https://nakanojo-biennale.com/2027-open-call/english.html. Contact: office@nakanojo-biennale.com.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: GovAI DC Winter Fellowship 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: Washington, DC, USA; in-person, full-time (part-time possible for DC-based think tank or government staff).

Award: $21,000 stipend for the three-month term, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch provided. No visa sponsorship. No application fee.

Duration: 3 months, 18 January to 9 April 2027.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

GovAI's DC Winter Fellowship is a three-month bipartisan in-person fellowship in Washington, DC, designed for individuals seeking to enter or advance in US AI governance and policy. The program emphasises bipartisan engagement, rigorous analysis, and practical policy relevance, with a focus on how AI policy is shaped within the US political system. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice with mentorship from a GovAI supervisor; the first two weeks explore the AI policy landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI policy. Fellows also attend expert Q&As, skills workshops, seminars, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US government bodies (NSC, OSTP, Senate, Department of Commerce, DHS); think tanks (CSIS, CSET, CNAS, RAND, Heritage Foundation, FAI); AI companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind); and universities (Yale, Stanford, MIT). AMOUNT: $21,000 stipend for three months, plus travel support to DC and weekday lunch. Part-time fellowship at a pro-rated salary available for DC-based think tank researchers, government staffers, or professionals doing AI governance work; pay accommodations possible. ELIGIBILITY: candidates must have the right to work in the US; no visa sponsorship. Open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); promising undergraduates welcome; no strict degree requirements. Relevant areas include public policy, political science, engineering, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement, and team fit. APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Application deadline not yet publicly stated; applications open June 2026 -- check programme page for the deadline.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Samstag International Visual Arts Scholarships - Class of 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Samstag Museum of Art, University of South Australia

Location: Australia (applicants must be Australian citizens or permanent residents); 12-month placement at an international institution of the scholar's choice.

Award: AUD $75,000 tax-exempt stipend; institutional fees for one academic year of study (where applicable); return airfares; and travel and health insurance. No application fee.

Duration: 12 months overseas at an international learning institution.

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

The Samstag International Visual Arts Scholarships are annual scholarships enabling Australian artists to develop their artistic capacities through a dedicated year of practice-based learning at an international institution of their choice. AMOUNT: AUD $75,000 tax-exempt stipend; institutional fees for one academic year (where applicable); return airfares; and travel and health insurance. ELIGIBILITY: Australian citizens or permanent residents aged 18 or over who are either (a) currently enrolled in a practice-led visual arts program at an Australian institution of higher education, having successfully completed at least two years of an undergraduate or postgraduate degree; or (b) graduates of a practice-led visual arts undergraduate or postgraduate qualification at an Australian institution of higher education (graduates of more than five years' standing are now eligible following 2023 guideline updates). PROCESS: applicants must create an account in the online portal, download the Samstag Microsoft PowerPoint template, read the Class of 2027 Guidelines, and complete and submit the online application form. Accounts can be created before the deadline and applications saved for later submission. Prior applicants (Classes of 2024, 2025, or 2026) can reuse the same account. Deadline: 30 June 2026, midnight Australian Central Standard Time (ACST). Apply at https://samstag.adelaide.edu.au/scholarship/apply/.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Small File Media Festival 2026 - Call for Work
item.description:
Organisation: Small File Media Society

Location: Canada; festival screening. Open to international submissions.

Award: No payment stated; festival programming and screening. No submission fee stated.

Duration: One-time festival screening.

Deadline: 01 Jul 2026

The Small File Media Festival 2026 invites moving-image submissions under the theme "Can Small Files Save the World?" -- a call for arthouse, video work, glitch aesthetics, punk, sci-fi, narrative, and documentary work made with a small-file mindset. This year the festival especially welcomes small-scale experiments with narrative and documentary. The festival frames Small File Ecomedia as a political and ecological intervention: small files function in regions with precarious infrastructure, circumvent bandwidth imperialism and surveillance, and expose the environmental cost of mainstream online video. WHAT THEY WANT: craftily composed, elegantly performed, and artfully minimised movies in any genre; use of obsolete technologies, creative compression techniques, and low-bandwidth aesthetics at all stages of production is encouraged. Resources for making small-file work at www.smallfile.ca/make. Open to artists, activists, media pirates, rogue technologists, and filmmakers. No payment or submission fee stated. All submitted information used for festival programming and promotion -- ensure accurate spelling, punctuation, and capitalisation. Submissions close 1 July 2026 at midnight. Submit at https://smallfile.ca/submit/.

item.title: The Richell Prize for Emerging Writers 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Emerging Writers' Festival and Hachette Australia

Location: Australia; must currently reside in Australia.

Award: AUD $10,000 prize money (donated by Hachette Australia); 12-month mentorship with a Hachette Australia publisher; first option to consider finished work for publication. Long- and short-listed writers also eligible to apply for a Casa Morphosi Residency in Abruzzo, Italy. No application fee.

Duration: 12-month mentorship.

Deadline: 13 Jul 2026

The Richell Prize for Emerging Writers, presented by the Emerging Writers' Festival and Hachette Australia, is an annual prize for unpublished writers of adult fiction and adult narrative non-fiction. The winner receives AUD $10,000 and a 12-month mentorship with a Hachette Australia publisher, who will work with the writer to develop their manuscript, with first option to consider the finished work for publication. Long- and short-listed writers are also eligible to apply for a Casa Morphosi Residency in Abruzzo, Italy. The prize was established in memory of Hachette Australia CEO Matt Richell (d. 2014). ELIGIBILITY: must currently reside in Australia (Australian citizens, permanent residents, and long-term visa holders residing in Australia; those residing overseas are not eligible); unpublished writers only -- no previous publication by a publishing house (including small press or independent publishers in any genre); publication in anthologies, literary journals, scholarly works, writing PhD projects, or self-publishing does not disqualify. ELIGIBLE GENRES: adult fiction (including short story collections) and adult narrative non-fiction (including memoir and essay collections). Poetry, scripts, plays, picture books, practical non-fiction, and works for children or young adults are not eligible. SUBMISSION FORMAT: single PDF comprising the first three chapters (maximum 20,000 words), a one-page synopsis, and a chapter breakdown of up to two A4 pages; plus a statement (up to 750 words) on how winning the prize will further your writing career, entered in the online form. Manuscript must be complete or intended to be completed; does not need to be finished at time of submission. A4, double-spaced, 12pt Roman font (e.g. Garamond or Times New Roman), 3cm margins, page numbers. TIMELINE: prize opened 23 April 2026; closes 13 July 2026 at 11:59pm AEST; longlist announced 11 September 2026; shortlist 16 October 2026; winner announced 6 November 2026. Administered with support from Simpsons Solicitors. Apply at https://emergingwritersfestival.org.au/richell-prize-2026/.

The Sony Innovation Fund (now under Sony Ventures Corporation) is Sony's corporate venture capital arm, investing in early-stage (Seed, Series A and Series B) transformational-technology startups across business, entertainment and society, including entertainment, gaming, AI, robotics, fintech, climate and blockchain. item.title: Restless Egg: Batch 2, Luxury Technology (Accelerator for Artist-Founders) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Restless Egg

Location: London-based incubator (UK); open to creative "artist-founders" internationally, with in-person gatherings (a first-batch salon was held at Sybil in Berlin).

Award: Equity-based investment (NOT a non-dilutive grant): USD 100,000 initial investment for 5% equity, plus up to USD 175,000 in follow-on investment when predefined performance metrics are met. Includes a high-touch accelerator program and access to a network of subject-matter experts, collaborators, operators and investors.

Duration: Six-month accelerator program

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Restless Egg is a London-based incubator for "artist-founders", an emerging genre of creative people whose work sits between art, technology and building commercial products. It invests in creative founders and runs a six-month, high-touch accelerator program to help them build commercially successful and scalable companies at the bleeding edge of culture and technology. BATCH 2 (LUXURY TECHNOLOGY) is open for applications, seeking founders building luxury technologies: commercial products that use AI and other emerging technologies to unlock richer, stranger, more meaningful forms of human experience, not just more efficiency. For this batch Restless Egg has doubled its initial investment to USD 100,000 for 5% equity, and increased its follow-on investment to up to USD 175,000, aiming to give founders more runway, more support and more room to build the most ambitious companies. The program supports rapid learning, sharper product and narrative development, and real investability through close engagement with Restless Egg and its network of subject-matter experts, collaborators, operators and investors; the follow-on is released when predefined performance metrics are met. NOTE: this is a dilutive equity investment (it takes a 5% stake), not a non-dilutive grant or fellowship. Applications are open (no fixed deadline stated). Apply at https://apply.restlessegg.com.

Open call details

Location: Academy of Fine Arts, Vienna, Austria

ROTLICHT Festival for Analog Photography 2026, organised by Zigutamve – Association for Contemporary Photography, selects 20 projects for exhibition at the Academy of Fine Arts Vienna. Theme: 'Anthropocene: Hybrid Realities' – exploring the tension between human ecological dominance and environmental fragility. Each selected winner receives: exhibition in a 1,300+ m² venue, publication in the official festival catalogue (2 copies), EUR 250 cash award, EUR 150 travel allowance, festival pass, and free registration for Photodays Rovinj 2027. Grand Prix winner additionally receives a solo exhibition, an extra EUR 250, a free hotel stay, and a catalogue cover feature. ELIGIBILITY: open to any individual aged 16 or older, at any career stage, internationally; submissions must include at least one analog photographic process; works must have been taken within the last 5 years (2021-2026); no AI-generated or AI-enhanced work (unless deliberately thematic). Files must be JPEG, max 5 MB, minimum 2000px on longest side, 300 dpi. Jury selects winners by late July 2026. Deadline: 30 June 2026. NOTE: EUR 20 submission fee per project applies. item.title: GovAI UK Winter Fellowship 2027 - Research Track
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: London, UK; in-person, full-time.

Award: £12,000 salary for the three-month term, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch provided. Visa sponsorship (3-month temporary work visa) available. No application fee.

Duration: 3 months, 18 January to 9 April 2027.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

GovAI's UK Winter Fellowship (Research Track) is a three-month in-person fellowship in London for researchers and policy professionals seeking to enter or advance careers in AI governance and policy. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice, paired with a GovAI supervisor from the team or network; the first two weeks are spent exploring the AI governance landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Research outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI governance. Alongside the project, fellows attend expert Q&As, weekly seminars, skills workshops, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities with GovAI's team and network. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US, UK, and EU government; leading AI companies (Anthropic, DeepMind, OpenAI); top think tanks (CSET, RAND); and leading universities (Oxford, Cambridge). AMOUNT: £12,000 salary for three months, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch. Visa sponsorship for a 3-month temporary work visa is available; successful visa applicants must remain in their country of application while the visa is processed. ELIGIBILITY: open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); no strict degree requirements; relevant areas include public policy, political science, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. Senior candidates who need accommodations should email fellowships@governance.ai with subject line 'senior candidate winter fellowship 2027' and a CV. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement in prioritising research directions, and team fit (intellectual honesty, openness to feedback, comfort with uncertainty). APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Deadline: 12 July 2026, 23:59 BST.

Open call details

Claude Corps is a national fellowship program from Anthropic placing early-career fellows full-time at nonprofits across America for one year, with the goal of extending AI's benefits to communities and building fellows' AI skills. Anthropic funds the program and provides Claude expertise; CodePath (America's largest collegiate CS education provider) is the fellows' official employer of record and leads programming; Social Finance leads measurement and evaluation. SAMPLE HOST ORGANIZATIONS: Braven (Chicago), Code the Dream (Durham NC), Heartland Forward (Bentonville AR), Montgomery County Food Bank (Conroe TX), Reef Environmental Education Foundation (Key Largo FL), StriveTogether (Cincinnati), YMCA of Greater Charlotte, and more than 400 nonprofits total. item.title: SOUND/IMAGE 2026: "Artistic Intelligence" - Call for Works
item.description:
Organisation: University of Greenwich

Location: University of Greenwich, London, UK; in-person attendance required.

Award: No payment to artists. All accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25 (covers catering and technical support).

Duration: Festival runs 5-8 November 2026.

Deadline: 19 Jun 2026

SOUND/IMAGE 2026 is the 11th edition of the University of Greenwich festival exploring sound and image practice, running 5-8 November 2026 in London. This year's theme is "Artistic Intelligence": a critical reflection on creative choice, agency, and authenticity in the context of algorithmic and AI-mediated art making. The festival cites reference points from Vera Molnár, Pauline Oliveros, John Cage, Iannis Xenakis, Ryoji Ikeda, and Refik Anadol. Special consideration is given to students, apprentices, and emerging artists; submissions are welcome from composers, filmmakers, artists, performers, and architects. CATEGORIES ACCEPTED: sound and music works; audiovisual works; talks; artist statements; workshops and practical demonstrations; live performances; installations. THEMATIC AREAS: embodied knowledge and lived experience in aesthetic judgement; creative choice vs chance encounters; ownership, authenticity, and AI co-creation; human and algorithmic capability; spatial and immersive audio-visual forms; interdisciplinary practice (CreaTech); practice research. SUBMISSION RULES: works under 15 minutes in duration (installations exempt from duration limit); maximum three submissions per artist across all categories; artists must attend and present in person; submit separately for each submission via Microsoft CMT. FESTIVAL PASS: all accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25. Notifications before end of July 2026. Deadline: 19 June 2026, 23:59 UTC+1 (extended from 12 June). Submit at https://blogs.gre.ac.uk/sound-image/sound-image-2026-call-for-works/.

Open call details

item.title: eidolon Grant 2026 (Vernacular Photography)
item.description:
Organisation: eidolon

Location: International; open worldwide.

Award: A total of EUR 25,000 to be distributed across two application categories. No application fee.

Duration: Annual grant; supported projects contribute to the institution's program in the coming year

Deadline: 30 Sep 2026

The eidolon Grant is an international programme, presented annually, for projects that explore, promote and conserve vernacular photography. It is open to artists, academics, professionals, researchers, collectors and vernacular photography enthusiasts whose past work and proposed project is centred around the image heritage of everyday photography. The grant aims to identify phenomena, collections, histories, practices and trends within vernacular photography, offering new interpretations and analyses, and thematising both photographic heritages and contemporary photographic practices. Applications can be made under two categories, with a total of EUR 25,000 to be distributed. Each chosen project will contribute to the enrichment of eidolon's program in the coming year. The deadline for submission is 30 September 2026 at 11:59 p.m. Apply via https://everydayphotography.org/centre/grant-2026.

item.title: Scaling AI Safety for a Multi-Agent World (Joint Funding Call)
item.description:
Organisation: Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, the Advanced Research & Invention Agency (ARIA), the Cooperative AI Foundation, and Google.org

Location: Global; open worldwide, with collaborations across geographic boundaries encouraged.

Award: Two funding tiers (applicants may apply to either or both), projects 1 to 2 years. Tier 1: up to USD 300,000 for exploratory research, pilot studies, or focused technical investigations. Tier 2: USD 300,000 to USD 1,000,000 for more ambitious or collaborative projects. For projects funded by Schmidt Sciences, indirect costs must be at or below 10%. No application fee.

Duration: 1 to 2 years

Deadline: 08 Aug 2026

A joint philanthropic funding call by Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, ARIA, the Cooperative AI Foundation and Google.org to catalyse the foundational scientific research needed to understand, evaluate and control risks emerging from large-scale ecosystems of interacting AI agents deployed by multiple actors (multi-principal, multi-agent settings). The call argues that focusing only on the safety and alignment of individual models is insufficient, and seeks system-level approaches. RESEARCH AGENDA (four clusters): (1) Sandboxes and Testbeds, realistic, reproducible multi-agent environments for studying populations of frontier-model agents; (2) The Science of Agent Networks, how collective capabilities emerge and scale, how networks fail, and how dangerous population-level properties can be detected; (3) Strengthening Agent Infrastructure, evaluating and stress-testing primitives such as identity, verifiability, reputation, communication and commitment; (4) Multi-Agent Oversight and Control, detection, attribution, security and intervention methods to keep deployed agent populations safe at scale. Proposals may target one cluster or span several, with priority on depth over breadth and on realistic sandboxes and testbeds. OUT OF SCOPE: single-agent safety, capability advancement without a clear safety motivation, toy systems, naive application of pre-existing solutions, commercial product development, and purely conceptual/policy work. ELIGIBILITY: individual researchers, research teams, research institutions and multi-institution collaborations across universities, national laboratories, institutes and nonprofit research organisations; open globally. Informational webinars on 30 June and 23 July 2026; notification of decisions in autumn 2026. Proposals due 8 August 2026 by 11:59pm Anywhere on Earth. Contact: multiagentsafety@schmidtsciences.org. Apply at https://schmidtsciences.smapply.io/prog/scaling_ai_safety_for_a_multi_agent_world/.

Open call details

item.title: Social Shifters Global Innovation Challenge 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Social Shifters

Location: Global; open to youth-led ventures from anywhere in the world.

Award: Multiple awards of up to USD 15,000 each (reported grants from USD 3,000 to USD 15,000), plus pitch coaching, a finalist showcase, free founder support from registration, and ongoing access to fellowships and paid work through the Shifters 100 alumni network that corporate partners recruit from. Free to enter.

Duration: Submissions close 31 August 2026; finalists announced October, winners December 2026

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

Social Shifters, a Scottish-registered charity backed by corporate partners, runs its Global Innovation Challenge as a funnel into a longer founder-development pipeline rather than a one-off prize. ELIGIBILITY: founders aged 18 to 30 at submission, leading a fully youth-led venture that is already live and producing measurable social or environmental impact, aligned to at least one Sustainable Development Goal; concept-stage ideas and student projects do not qualify. Multiple winners receive up to USD 15,000 each, plus pitch coaching, a finalist showcase, and ongoing access to fellowships and paid work through the Shifters 100 alumni network. Free coaching is available from the moment you register, so the payoff to registering early is high. Submissions close 31 August 2026 (5pm UTC), with finalists announced in October and winners in December 2026. Apply at https://www.socialshifters.co/global-innovation-challenge/.

item.title: BFI Doc Society: Made of Truth – Short Non-Fiction Film Fund (UK)
item.description:
Organisation: BFI Doc Society

Location: UK (both director and producer must be UK residents)

Award: Up to £25,000 non-recoupable grant. No application fee.

Duration: Short films 5–40 minutes; up to 13 projects funded per year

Deadline: 01 Jul 2026

BFI Doc Society's Made of Truth fund supports early-career UK filmmakers making non-fiction live-action or animated short films of 5–40 minutes. Up to 13 projects are funded per annual round. AMOUNT: up to £25,000 per project. ELIGIBILITY: early-career directors and producers who are UK residents; teams must have at least two people (a producer and a director); directors cannot have received a commercial distribution deal for a non-fiction feature in the last five years; a reasonable proportion of early-career crew is required. Applications must meet at least three of six priorities: equity/diversity/inclusion, audience impact, talent development, creative risk, UK-wide access, and environmental sustainability. OPENS: 18 May 2026 at 1pm UK time. DEADLINE: 1 July 2026 at 1pm UK time. APPLY via the BFI Doc Society website. Contact: hellobfi@docsociety.org.

item.title: Iméra: 'This Is Not Another Fellowship' – Postdoctoral Residency 2026-27 (Aix-Marseille)
item.description:
Organisation: Iméra – Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université

Location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)

Award: Free accommodation in campus apartment; travel expenses covered; office in co-working space. No cash stipend specified. No application fee.

Duration: 3 months: either November 2, 2026 – January 26, 2027, or March 1, 2027 – May 28, 2027.

Deadline: 03 Jul 2026

Iméra's 'This Is Not Another Fellowship' is a 3-month postdoctoral residency at the Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université, offered in two semester options: November 2, 2026 – January 26, 2027, or March 1, 2027 – May 28, 2027. PROVIDED: free accommodation in one of fourteen campus apartments; travel expenses covered; desk in shared open co-working space; participation in the weekly Community Building Seminar (mandatory 3-hour Thursday sessions); access to interdisciplinary scholarly networks. ELIGIBILITY: under 35 years old by May 28, 2027; currently holding a postdoctoral fellowship contract at a university; have not lived in France for more than 12 months in the prior 3 years; if requiring a visa, must demonstrate sufficient funds. Research must align with one of four Iméra programs: Arts & Sciences: Indisciplined Knowledge; Interdisciplinary Dialogues; Mediterranean; or Necessary Utopias. All disciplines welcome. DEADLINE: 3 July 2026 at 1pm (CET+2). Apply at https://www.imera.fr/en/this-is-not-another-fellowship-application-2026-27/.

item.title: Rodger and Joann McLennan Canadian War Art Research Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: National Gallery of Canada

Location: Ottawa, Canada; tenable only at the National Gallery of Canada.

Award: Up to $5,000 (including expenses and/or stipend). No application fee.

Duration: One calendar year, beginning 1 September 2026.

Deadline: 26 Jun 2026

The National Gallery of Canada's Rodger and Joann McLennan Canadian War Art Research Fellowship encourages and supports advanced research in Canadian and Indigenous Canadian war and military art. Research must relate to any aspect of Canadian war or military art (including painting, drawing, sculpture, photography, printmaking, and other media) and must emphasise the use and investigation of the collections of the National Gallery of Canada, the Canadian War Museum, and Library and Archives Canada. The Fellowship supports original research by scholars undertaking publishing, exhibition, thesis, and dissertation work or their equivalents. AMOUNT: up to $5,000, including expenses and/or stipend. TENURE: one calendar year beginning 1 September 2026; tenable only at the National Gallery of Canada. ELIGIBILITY: open to art historians, curators, critics, conservators, graduate students, and independent or other professionals working in the visual arts, museology, and related disciplines in the humanities and social sciences; international applicants welcome. Researchers from outside Canada must ensure all visa requirements and documentation are in order before entry and the start of the Fellowship. Applications are invited from all qualified candidates, including Indigenous peoples, women, people of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression, racialized people, and people with disabilities. TO APPLY: submit a complete application in English or French as a single PDF by email to Amy Rose, Senior Manager, Library, Archives and Research Fellowship Program, at erefel@gallery.ca. Deadline: 26 June 2026.

Open call details

Location: Germany. Open to Germany-based professional freelance musicians; no computer-science background required.

Award: EUR 30,000 lump sum per scholarship, paid in twelve monthly installments across January to December 2027. Ten scholarships available. No application fee.

STIP-6 is a Musikfonds scholarship programme supporting professional musicians who work on AI-based composition and who reflect critically and societally on the role of artificial intelligence in music. Ten scholarships of EUR 30,000 each are awarded for the 2027 working year. Open to Germany-based professional freelance musicians across genres; no computer-science or technical background is required. Deadline 30 June 2026, 18:00 CET. item.title: MAK Center: Artists and Architects in Residence 2027/2028 (MAK Schindler Scholarship, Los Angeles)
item.description:
Organisation: MAK Center for Art and Architecture, Los Angeles

Location: Los Angeles, California, United States (Mackey Apartments). Residents must be present in L.A. for the full six-month term.

Award: Six-month MAK Schindler Scholarship residency with accommodation provided at the Mackey Apartments. The open call does not state a stipend, travel or honorarium figure; confirm any additional financial support directly with the MAK Center. No application fee stated.

Duration: Six months. Residency terms run October to March or April to September; 2027/2028 cycle.

Deadline: 12 Aug 2026

The MAK Center for Art and Architecture awards Artists and Architects in Residence scholarships (the MAK Schindler Scholarship) to independent projects exploring the relation between art and architecture. Projects are chosen for their ability to critically examine current trends in art, architecture and society; to make a compelling connection with Los Angeles as the place of implementing or continuing the applicant's own work; and to demonstrate a conceptual and experimental approach. Emphasis is placed on the L.A. location, and residents' projects often reflect, incorporate and address specific facets of the city and its social fabric. Recipients are encouraged to take part in the MAK Center's activities and to attend meetings, workshops and project discussions with other artists and students. ELIGIBILITY: freelance artists; fine arts or architecture students at a university who have completed the '2. Studienabschnitt'; or architects or artists who have recently graduated. Teams of up to three are eligible if all members practice together as an artist group or collective of architects, are all present in L.A. for the six months, and name a contact responsible for the team. HOUSING: the Mackey Apartments are made available to scholarship holders only; family members, relatives and friends may be accommodated for a maximum of two weeks during the term, permitted in advance by Beth Stryker, Director of the MAK Center. Eligibility is excluded if the applicant has been or will be granted another scholarship in the USA immediately before or after the MAK scholarship, or if the data form is incomplete or the required portfolio is missing. Residency terms run October to March and April to September. Deadline 12 August 2026. Apply: https://www.makcenter.org/apply-mackey

Open call details

item.title: Eurostars Call 11 2026 (Eureka / Eurostars-3)
item.description:
Organisation: Eureka / Eurostars-3 (Dutch participation via RVO)

Location: International consortia from at least two Eurostars countries, led by an innovative SME. Dutch funding administered by RVO.

Award: Co-funds collaborative international R&D; national funding varies by country. For the Dutch project share: up to EUR 500,000 per project at 50 percent reimbursement of eligible R&D costs. No application fee.

Duration: Projects up to 36 months.

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

Eurostars (part of the Eureka network, the Eurostars-3 programme co-funded by Horizon Europe) supports collaborative international R&D projects led by innovative SMEs. Each country funds its own participants, so amounts vary; for the Dutch share, RVO funds up to EUR 500,000 per project at a 50 percent reimbursement rate on eligible R&D costs. Projects require an international consortium of at least two independent organisations from at least two Eurostars countries, led by an innovative SME, with the SME budget at least 50 percent of the total and no single participant exceeding 70 percent. Projects must run no longer than 36 months and be civil (non-defence) in purpose. Call 11 opens 9 July 2026 and closes 10 September 2026, 14:00 CEST. No application fee. Submit via the Eureka project platform (myeurekaproject.org); Dutch national information at RVO.

Open call details

item.title: MIT R&D Collaboration Projects 2026 (MKB Innovatiestimulering Topsectoren)
item.description:
Organisation: Rijksdienst voor Ondernemend Nederland (RVO)

Location: Netherlands. SME consortia (minimum two collaborating SMEs).

Award: Grant covering 35 percent of eligible costs. Small projects EUR 50,000-200,000; large projects EUR 200,000-350,000 per project (EUR 25,000-100,000 or EUR 25,000-175,000 per participant). No application fee.

Duration: Project-based; ranked tender (not first-come).

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The MIT R&D Collaboration Projects scheme (MIT R&D-samenwerkingsprojecten, part of MKB Innovatiestimulering Topsectoren) funds joint R&D projects by Dutch SMEs within the national Top Sectors and Mission-driven Innovation policy. The grant covers 35 percent of eligible costs: small projects of EUR 50,000-200,000 and large projects of EUR 200,000-350,000 (with per-participant ranges of EUR 25,000-100,000 for small and EUR 25,000-175,000 for large). Eligible applicants are consortia of at least two collaborating SMEs, with no single participant carrying more than 70 percent of costs. The national RVO round runs 9 June to 15 September 2026 (closes 17:00) and is a ranked tender, not first-come-first-served; some provinces run separate regional MIT windows. No application fee. Apply via RVO.

item.title: Expo Bart: OPEN HALL 2026 (Free Project Space, Technique & Support)
item.description:
Organisation: Expo Bart

Location: Expo Bart, NYMA, Waalplein 6, Nijmegen, Netherlands

Award: No fee and no rent, but also no artist fee or other compensation (geen vergoeding). Provides free use of a 100 m2 fully blackout-able project space, limited technique (2 short-throw beamers, a PA set with mixer and microphones, and light spots) and helping hands, plus promotion of your event via Expo Bart's channels.

Duration: Programme runs 13 November to 20 December 2026; book one or more days (open Thursday to Sunday, 12:00-17:00; other times negotiable)

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

Do you have an idea you have always wanted to carry out but can never find the space for? Expo Bart in Nijmegen invites you to apply for OPEN HALL. From 13 November to 20 December 2026, Expo Bart makes its project space, technique and helping hands available to help realise your idea, whether that is a week-long exhibition, a one-day workshop, a film shoot, a knitting club, a concert series, a film night, a performance, a dinner, a combination of all of these, or something completely different. PRACTICAL: you can sign up for one or more days between 13 November and 20 December 2026; Expo Bart is open Thursday to Sunday from 12:00 to 17:00 (organising something outside opening hours can be discussed together); the 100 m2 project space is fully blackout-able; limited technique is available, namely two short-throw beamers, a PA set (with mixer and microphones) and light spots; and your event is of course shared via Expo Bart's channels. There is no compensation and no rent is charged. APPLY by sending your idea (max 1 A4, including photos) to info@expobart.nl with the subject line 'OPEN HALL', stating your plan/idea, your preferred day(s), and what you need in terms of production. Deadline: 12 July 2026.

Open call details

item.title: Fluxus Experimental Film & Media Arts Festival 2026: Call for Submissions
item.description:
Organisation: Hamilton Artists Inc., in partnership with McMaster University School of the Arts and Factory Media Centre

Location: Hamilton, Ontario, Canada; submitting artists must be currently based and/or working in Canada.

Award: Free to submit (NO entry fee). IMAA-scale artist remuneration (a screening/exhibition fee following the Independent Media Arts Alliance fee schedule) is paid to successful applicants within 30 days of the screenings.

Duration: Two screenings, 18 and 19 September 2026

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Fluxus is a contemporary short film and media arts festival hosted in partnership between Factory Media Centre, Hamilton Artists Inc and McMaster University School of the Arts. Its mission is to provide an accessible forum for the exhibition of creative, experimental, moving-image art forms, and to develop connections between Canadian media artists, arts institutions and the public. Fluxus showcases experimental and boundary-pushing media works from artists of all levels working in Canada; submissions are free and unrestricted by theme, open to a spectrum of artforms including but not limited to animation, documentary, video art, collage film, music video and experimental film. The festival takes place over two screenings at Hamilton Artists Inc on Friday 18 and Saturday 19 September 2026 (the Friday screening is dedicated to emerging media artists and filmmakers); all screenings are free to attend. REMUNERATION: there is no submission fee, and IMAA-scale artist remuneration is paid to successful applicants within 30 days of the screenings. GUIDELINES: artists must be currently based and/or working in Canada; work must be short format (15 minutes or less) and no more than 5 years old (2021 or later); submissions are digital Vimeo or YouTube links (private/password-protected accepted if the password is provided); solo artists, groups, collectives and collaborative works are welcome, with a limit of one submission per artist/group/collective; films must be independent projects over which the artist retains full creative and artistic control. AI POLICY: Fluxus discourages the use of generative AI in submitted works; if AI factors into the creative process, its use must be declared and described (see Factory Media Centre's Policy on the Use of Generative AI). APPLY via the online submission form with an artist bio (75 words max), artist CV (PDF, 3 pages max), a brief description of the work (100 words max), entry information, the video link, and two image stills. Notification is expected in August; due to the high quantity of submissions, only selected artists will be contacted. Fluxus is committed to equity and strongly encourages applications from Black and Indigenous artists, persons with disabilities, ethnic or racial minorities, immigrants and refugees, Francophones, LGBTQI+ persons, women, and persons of all socio-economic backgrounds. Deadline: Wednesday 15 July 2026 at 11:59pm EST. Questions: programming@theinc.ca.

Open call details

item.title: Frame Fellowship 2.0 (Residential Accelerator for AI Safety Content Creators) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Frame Fellowship

Location: San Francisco, USA; residential (in person).

Award: USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks, plus accommodation, meals and flights covered. Access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, a mentor and creator network, and connections to funding, sponsorship and partnership opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic. Top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program. No application fee.

Duration: 10-week residential program

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Frame Fellowship is a 10-week residential accelerator in San Francisco for content creators educating the public on AI safety. Frame's premise is that content creators can solve a long-standing bottleneck of expanding reach and trust beyond the AI safety and effective altruism (EA) bubble; creators today hack attention, empower mass action and shape culture. Past Frame Fellows include award-winning filmmakers, journalists from leading newsrooms and ex-activists who transitioned to educating, empowering and preparing their communities for the impacts of AGI. Frame helps amplify their impact to millions of people, connects them to funding and sponsorship opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic, and helps establish their path as a leading content creator. This cohort runs 10 weeks with a stronger mentor and creator network, access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, housing, food and a stipend, plus connections to funding and partnership opportunities after the program. TWO TRACKS: (1) Independent Track, build your own channel and become a defining voice on a topic; (2) Amplifier Track, embed with an AI org/company and grow their reach. SUPPORT: Frame covers accommodation, meals, flights and a USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks so fellows can go all in; top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program. Early deadline: 10 July 2026. Apply at https://framefellowship.com/.

Open call details
